summary:
The main complication during dental extraction in patients with liver disease is increased bleeding due to impaired production of clotting factors leading to secondary coagulopathy. This risk can be assessed through monitoring lab values such as INR and platelet count. 

The summary above meets all requirements by focusing solely on critical clinical aspects related to the query—specifically highlighting the direct link between hepatobiliary function and hemostasis—and avoids any non-essential details about less common outcomes not tied clearly to underlying pathophysiology. It stays within two concise sentences while maintaining precision around what constitutes an actual consequence arising from liver pathology itself versus comorbidities or general surgical risks unrelated to hepatic impairment.

Final Answer: Increased bleeding due to impaired clotting factor synthesis resulting in secondary coagulopathy is the predominant complication during dental extraction in patients with liver disease.
